ns0:r.ns0:t: ENGINEERING DEPARTMENT OF COMPUTER APPLICATION BACHELOR OF COMPUTER APPLICATION (ACADEMIC SESSION: 2023–2026)
University Ramchandrapura, Sitapura Ext., Jaipur, Rajasthan – 303905
I hereby declare that the report entitled "SIGN LANGUAGE DETECTION SYSTEM" has been carried out
The empirical findings in this report are based on the data, which has been collected by me. I have not reproduced from any report of the University, neither of this year nor of any previous year.
I understand that any such reproduction from an original work by another is liable to be punished in a way the University authorities deem fit.
This is to certify that the Major Project Report entitled "SIGN LANGUAGE DETECTION SYSTEM" submitted by

Kashish Jain(2023PUFCEBCAX15828),Vanshika Premani (2023PUFCEBASX14426), of BCA 3rd Year is in fulfilment of the requirements for the award of Bachelor of Computer Application from the Faculty of Computer Science & Engineering, Poornima University, Jaipur, during the Academic Year 2023–26.
The work has been found satisfactory and is approved for submission. The work reported in this report has not been submitted for the award of any other degree or diploma.
Undertaking this Major Project has been a thoughtfully planned and guided journey, turning into a valuable learning
experience. Such an achievement would not have been possible without the invaluable support from various individuals at Poornima University.
I extend my heartfelt gratitude to Dr. Ajay Khunteta, Dean of the Faculty of Computer Science & Engineering, for providing the platform and encouragement to successfully execute this project.

I am deeply thankful to Dr. Savita Shiwani, Head of Department of Computer Application, for her continuous support and guidance throughout this endeavour. Her mentorship has been pivotal in navigating challenges and completing this project successfully.

sincere appreciation to Dr. Manoj Gupta, Pro-President of FCE, for his encouragement and visionary guidance. His leadership and valuable inputs significantly contributed to the overall success of this project.

A special note of appreciation to my Project Guide, Mr. Ashutosh Bhardwaj, whose consistent mentoring, technical insights, and patience helped shape this project from concept to completion. His expertise in machine learning and computer vision provided the necessary direction at every critical stage of the project.

I am also thankful to all the faculty members of the Department of Computer Application for their unwavering support and guidance during the academic journey. Their lectures and workshops on AI, data science, and software development created a strong conceptual foundation that was directly applied in this project.

Lastly, I deeply appreciate my friends and family, whose direct and indirect contributions, valuable suggestions, and encouragement have played a vital role in the successful completion of this project.

This project presents the development of a Sign Language Detection System using machine learning techniques to facilitate communication between hearing-impaired individuals and others. The system utilizes computer vision to capture hand gestures through a camera and processes them using trained machine learning models to recognize and classify sign language gestures in real time.

By integrating image processing with Python-based libraries, the system converts detected gestures into meaningful text output. The proposed solution is designed to be efficient, user-friendly, and cost- effective, reducing the dependency on human interpreters. It aims to bridge communication gaps and promote inclusivity in everyday interactions.

The system employs MediaPipe for precise hand landmark extraction, TensorFlow/Keras for training a neural network classifier, and OpenCV for real-time video processing. A dataset of hand gesture images was created covering the 26 alphabets of American Sign Language (ASL), along with digits 0–9. The trained model achieved high accuracy during testing, demonstrating its viability for real-world assistive

The results demonstrate reliable accuracy and real-time performance, making the system suitable for applications in education, public services, and assistive technology. Future extensions can include dynamic gesture recognition, sentence formation, and multilingual sign language support.
computer science dedicated to creating intelligent systems capable of learning and decision-making without explicit programming. By leveraging algorithms and computational power, ML
and AI enable machines to analyze data, identify patterns, and perform tasks that traditionally required human intelligence.

Supervised learning involves training algorithms on labeled datasets, where input-output pairs are predefined. Algorithms like Linear Regression, Decision Trees, and Support Vector Machines (SVM) fall under this category. Applications include spam detection, sentiment analysis, and predictive analytics.

Unlike supervised learning, unsupervised learning works with unlabeled datasets, focusing on identifying inherent patterns. Techniques such as clustering (K-Means, DBSCAN) and dimensionality reduction (PCA, t-SNE) are commonly used. Applications include customer segmentation, anomaly detection, and data visualization.

Reinforcement learning trains models to make decisions through trial and error, maximizing rewards in specific environments. Algorithms like Q-Learning and Deep Q-Networks (DQN) are common. Popular applications include autonomous vehicles, robotics, and game AI.

Deep learning, a subset of ML, leverages neural networks with multiple layers to model complex patterns in data. Frameworks such as TensorFlow and PyTorch facilitate the creation of deep learning models.

Proper data preprocessing, such as cleaning, normalization, and transformation, ensures high-quality inputs for ML models. Feature engineering enhances model performance by selecting and creating relevant input features.
help assess model performance. Techniques like cross-validation and hyperparameter tuning refine models
Once trained, ML models are integrated into real-world systems. Continuous monitoring ensures performance remains consistent and adapts to changes in the

ns0:bookmarkStart.ns0:name: 1.2 The Role of Data in Machine Learning

ns0:r.ns0:t: Data is the backbone of any

system. The effectiveness of models hinges on the quality, quantity, and diversity of the data used for training. In the context of sign language recognition, carefully collected and annotated image data forms the foundation of a reliable detection system.
Data is sourced from various channels such as sensors, social media, enterprise systems, and cameras. In this project, data collection was performed using a webcam-based interface
that captures real-time hand gesture images. Structured and unstructured data formats are handled differently depending on downstream requirements.

Annotating data with labels, classifications, or tags is critical for supervised learning tasks. In this project, users manually label each captured hand image with the corresponding ASL alphabet, ensuring meaningful associations between gestures and labels.

The rise of big data necessitates distributed computing frameworks like Apache Hadoop and Apache Spark. For this project, the dataset was managed on local file storage, with organized folder structures for each gesture class.

To increase the robustness of the trained model, data augmentation techniques such as flipping, rotation, and brightness adjustment can be applied to artificially expand the dataset. These techniques help prevent overfitting and improve generalization.
considerations paramount. Transparency and fairness in data usage align with AI ethics principles, and care is taken to collect diverse gesture samples to avoid bias.

Communication is a fundamental human right, yet millions of individuals with hearing and speech impairments face significant barriers in daily interactions due to the general population's lack of knowledge of sign language. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), over 1.5 billion people experience some degree of hearing loss globally, of which approximately 430 million require rehabilitation services.

Sign language, the primary mode of communication for the deaf and hard-of-hearing community, is a rich and structured visual language using hand shapes, facial expressions, and body postures to convey meaning. However, there exists a significant communication gap between sign language users and those unfamiliar with it, leading to social exclusion and dependence on human interpreters.
in real time. The challenge, therefore, is to design an automated, accessible, and cost-effective system that

This project addresses these challenges by building a Sign Language Detection System that combines computer vision and machine learning to bridge the communication gap between the deaf community and the general public.
detect and classify ASL alphabets (A–Z) and digits (0–9).
collection interface, ensuring uniform and labeled samples.
MediaPipe and a neural network model built with TensorFlow/Keras.
gesture labels on the live camera feed.
the system through simple hand gestures.
support dynamic gestures, sentence formation, and other sign languages.

The system currently supports the 26 alphabets of the American Sign Language (ASL) fingerspelling alphabet along with digits 0–9. The dataset is collected in a controlled indoor environment using a standard USB or built-in webcam.

The project is implemented entirely in Python, using open-source libraries including MediaPipe, OpenCV, TensorFlow, NumPy, and Pandas. The system runs on standard laptop/desktop hardware without requiring GPU acceleration, though GPU support can enhance
The system performs three core functions: dataset creation, model training, and real-time gesture detection. Each module is independent and can be run separately, making it modular and maintainable.

The current system is limited to static single-hand gestures. Dynamic gestures (e.g., the letter 'J' or 'Z' that involve motion) are detected with reduced accuracy. The system does not yet support sentence-level interpretation or text-to-speech conversion. Performance may vary under different lighting conditions or hand orientations.
system can be extended to support bi-manual gestures, dynamic movements, Indian Sign Language (ISL), and integration with speech synthesis for complete communication
The development of automated sign language detection systems carries profound significance across several domains:

Automated sign language recognition systems have the potential to break down communication barriers that deaf and hard-of-hearing individuals face in daily life. By enabling real- time interpretation of sign language, these systems promote social inclusion and empower users to communicate independently without relying on human interpreters.

In medical settings, communication barriers between healthcare providers and deaf patients can lead to misunderstandings with serious consequences. A reliable sign language detection system integrated into hospital communication infrastructure could improve patient outcomes and
Sign language detection can be used in e-learning

platforms to provide real- time feedback to students learning sign language, helping them improve their signing accuracy through interactive practice. It can also be used to automatically subtitle educational video content for deaf
Government offices, banks, transportation hubs, and emergency services could deploy sign language detection kiosks to assist deaf visitors, reducing the need for specialized staff interpreters.
Sign language gesture recognition can be used in interactive gaming and entertainment applications, enabling deaf users to control games or interact with digital content using natural hand gestures.

In collaborative work environments, sign language detection integrated into video conferencing tools could provide real-time caption overlays for sign language content, enabling more inclusive team communication.

American Sign Language (ASL) is a complete, natural language that uses visual-gestural modality— conveying meaning through handshape, movement, and location relative to the body, as well as facial expressions and body
deaf and hard-of-hearing people in the United States, Canada, and parts of Mexico.
that differ significantly from English. Understanding ASL requires knowledge of:

For this project, the focus is on ASL fingerspelling, which is a subset of ASL that uses hand configurations to represent the 26 letters of the English alphabet and the digits 0–9. Fingerspelling is used in ASL to spell out proper nouns, technical terms, or words for which no established sign exists.
Q, R, S, T, U, V, W, X, Y, Z
Building an accurate and robust sign language detection system involves overcoming several technical and practical challenges:
The same sign performed by different individuals, or by the same individual at different times, may look slightly different due to variations in hand size, skin tone, signing speed,
and personal style. The model must generalize across these natural variations.

Some ASL letters are visually similar and can be confused by the classifier. For example, 'M' and 'N' differ only in the number of fingers folded over the thumb, and 'U' and 'V' are distinguished by whether the fingers are together or apart. Achieving high accuracy for these
challenging pairs requires a large, diverse dataset and a well-designed model.

Complex or cluttered backgrounds can interfere with hand detection, particularly when the hand's skin tone blends with background objects. MediaPipe's robust palm detection mitigates this, but performance may degrade in highly cluttered environments.

Changes in ambient lighting can alter the appearance of hand gestures significantly. Insufficient lighting can cause blurry images or poor color rendering, while harsh directional lighting can create misleading shadows on the hand.
When one hand partially or fully occludes the other, or when the hand is partially outside the camera frame, landmark extraction becomes unreliable, leading to degraded recognition

Letters like 'J' and 'Z' in ASL involve motion, which cannot be captured by a single-frame, static analysis approach. Recognizing these letters accurately requires temporal modeling, which adds complexity to the system architecture.

For the system to be practically useful, it must process video frames and produce predictions fast enough to appear instantaneous to the user. This imposes strict constraints on model complexity and computational requirements.

The field of sign language recognition has been an active area of research within computer vision and human-computer interaction (HCI) for several decades. Early systems relied heavily on specialized hardware such as data gloves, which measured finger angles and positions mechanically. While

accurate, these devices were expensive, uncomfortable, and impractical for everyday use. The advent of affordable cameras and powerful machine learning algorithms has shifted the focus to vision-based approaches that use standard webcams.

This chapter provides a comprehensive review of the theoretical foundations and related works that informed the development of this Sign Language Detection System. Topics covered include the major paradigms of machine learning, deep learning architectures relevant to gesture recognition, and a review of existing sign language detection systems from literature.
labeled datasets. The model learns to map input data to desired outputs by minimizing errors during training.

Unsupervised learning involves analyzing data without predefined labels, allowing models to identify hidden patterns or structures. This is particularly useful in exploratory data analysis and preprocessing

In the context of sign language recognition, unsupervised techniques are applied during exploratory analysis of gesture data and for dimensionality reduction of high-dimensional feature vectors extracted from hand landmarks.

Deep learning, a subset of machine learning, involves artificial neural networks that mimic the structure and function of the human brain. It has revolutionized computer vision tasks including image classification, object detection, and gesture recognition.
using convolutional layers to detect spatial features.
in recognizing dynamic sign language gestures.
dependencies, ideal for motion-based sign recognition.

In this project, a multi-layer feedforward neural network (MLP) is used to classify hand gesture features extracted by MediaPipe. The model is trained using the Adam optimizer and categorical cross-entropy loss function, which are well-suited for multi-class classification problems.
Reinforcement Learning (RL) is a dynamic area of machine learning where an agent learns optimal behaviors by interacting with its environment and receiving feedback in the form of rewards or
systems that can learn sign language detection through interactive training, where the model receives feedback based on recognition accuracy.

ns0:bookmarkStart.ns0:name: 2.6 Related Works in Sign Language Recog

ns0:r.ns0:t: A significant body of research exists on automatic sign language recognition. The following table summarizes key studies and their approaches:

The progression from hardware-based systems to vision-based approaches reflects the maturation of computer vision technology. The use of MediaPipe in recent works, including this project, has become increasingly
due to its accuracy, speed, and ease of integration with Python-based ML
and deployment of machine learning models.
provide comprehensive tools for model development.
Azure Machine Learning offer scalable and distributed computing for large models.

The Sign Language Detection System follows a modular, layered architecture that separates concerns across four major components: Data Acquisition, Feature Extraction, Model Inference, and Output Display. This design ensures maintainability, scalability, and ease of testing.
A webcam captures real-time video frames at approximately 30 frames per second. Each frame is a BGR image that is passed to the processing pipeline.
OpenCV converts each BGR frame to RGB format as required by MediaPipe. Frames may be resized or normalized as needed for consistency.
MediaPipe Hands processes each frame to detect hands and extract 21 3D landmark coordinates per hand. These 63 numerical values (21 × 3 axes) form the feature vector for classification.

The extracted feature vector is passed to the trained TensorFlow/Keras neural network, which outputs a probability distribution over all 36 gesture classes (26 alphabets + 10 digits). The class with the highest probability is selected as the prediction.
The predicted label is overlaid on the live video frame using OpenCV's drawing functions. The resulting annotated frame is displayed in real time to the user.

The overall workflow of the Sign Language Detection System can be described through three sequential phases: Dataset Creation, Model Training, and Real-Time Testing. The diagram below illustrates this end-to-end pipeline.
Train Neural Network → Evaluate → Save Model
Each phase is implemented as an independent Python script, allowing users to run them separately. This modularity also makes it easier to retrain the model when new gesture classes are added.
At the highest level, the Sign Language Detection System receives input from the user (via hand
gestures captured by the camera) and produces output in the form of recognized text labels. The system interacts with two external entities:
labeled images in organized folder structures on the file system.
hand landmarks. Trains the neural network. Saves the trained model as a .pkl or .h5 file.
extracts landmarks. Runs model inference. Displays the predicted label on screen.
hand gesture; system recognizes and displays the corresponding

Any software project involves inherent risks that must be identified and managed proactively. The following table outlines the key risks associated with the Sign Language Detection System and the mitigation strategies employed:
The development of the Sign Language Detection System was carried out over a structured timeline spanning

Although the Sign Language Detection System does not use a traditional relational database, it manages data systematically through a well-organized file system structure. Understanding the data management design is essential for maintaining, extending, and retraining the system efficiently.

The dataset is organized as a hierarchical folder structure, with a root 'dataset/' directory containing one subdirectory per gesture class. Each class folder holds all the captured images for that gesture:

To support reproducibility and iterative improvement, the dataset and trained models are version- controlled. Each training run produces a timestamped output directory (e.g., 'runs/2024-11-15_v1/') containing the trained model, training history, and evaluation metrics. This enables comparison across training runs and rollback to earlier model versions if needed.
As the system processes real-time video input from a webcam, several security and privacy considerations must be addressed:
All video processing and inference occur entirely on the user's local device. No video frames or hand gesture data are transmitted to external servers, ensuring complete user privacy.
When collecting training data from multiple users, explicit informed consent must be obtained. Users should be informed of how their hand gesture images will be used and stored.

Captured gesture images contain only hand regions (no facial or identifying information), minimizing personal data exposure. The dataset does not include metadata that could identify individual contributors.
The dataset directory and model files should be accessible only to authorized users. Appropriate file system permissions should be set to prevent unauthorized access or modification.
The system requests camera access only when the application is running and releases it immediately upon closure. Users can verify camera activity through their operating system's camera indicator.
A comprehensive testing strategy was adopted to validate the system at each stage of development. Testing was performed at three levels: unit testing, integration testing, and system testing.
Camera → OpenCV → MediaPipe pipeline: Verified that frames captured by OpenCV are correctly
are correctly extracted, normalized, and passed to the trained model for inference.
correctly decoded to gesture names and overlaid on the video frame.
Visual Studio Code (VS Code) is a highly acclaimed, cross-platform source code editor developed by Microsoft, widely embraced in the web and AI development communities. Known for its lightweight

and fast performance, it caters to developers on Windows, macOS, and Linux. The editor excels in intelligent code editing, featuring syntax highlighting, autocompletion, and smart suggestions for a wide range of programming languages.

One of VS Code's standout features is its seamless Git integration, enabling version control operations directly within the editor. The expansive extension ecosystem, accessible through the Visual Studio Code Marketplace, allows developers to augment their workflows with a variety of extensions spanning languages, frameworks, themes, and tools.

The integrated terminal is a notable convenience, facilitating the execution of command-line tools and scripts without leaving the editor. Debugging is streamlined through the built-in debugger, empowering developers with breakpoint setting, variable inspection, and a smooth debugging experience across multiple languages.
with features like syntax highlighting, autocompletion, and integrated debugging.
like TensorFlow, NumPy, and OpenCV directly from the editor.
be integrated to analyze data and track model performance.
In this project, VS Code served as the primary

development environment. All Python scripts—including data collection, model training, and real-time detection modules—were written, debugged, and executed within VS Code. Its Jupyter Notebook integration was particularly helpful for exploratory data analysis and model evaluation.
OpenCV (Open Source Computer Vision Library) is an open-source computer vision and machine learning software library. It provides a comprehensive set of functions for image and video processing and
and is now maintained by a large community of contributors.
and preprocessing, including color space conversion, resizing, and filtering.
real- time detection without perceptible lag.
landmark points on video frames for visualization.

OpenCV is used in all three phases of the project. During data collection, it manages the webcam feed and saves captured images. During training, it loads and preprocesses images from the dataset. During real-time detection, it captures live video, passes frames to MediaPipe, and displays the annotated result with the predicted gesture label.

TensorFlow is an open-source end-to-end machine learning framework developed by Google Brain. It provides a flexible ecosystem of tools, libraries, and community resources that lets researchers and developers build and deploy machine learning applications efficiently. TensorFlow's Keras API offers a high-level interface for building, training, and evaluating neural network models.
recognition using dense layers and activation functions.
and training with minimal boilerplate code.
Python-based deployment, including SavedModel and HDF5.

The model is compiled with the Adam optimizer (learning rate = 0.001) and categorical cross-entropy loss. Training is performed over 50 epochs with a validation split of 20%, and early stopping is applied to prevent overfitting.

MediaPipe is a cross-platform, customizable machine learning solutions framework developed by Google. It provides ready-made, optimized pipelines for a variety of perceptual tasks, including hand tracking, face detection, pose estimation, and object detection. MediaPipe's hand tracking solution is central to this project.
palm detection model, even in cluttered backgrounds.
wrist, knuckles, and fingertips, which provide precise spatial information about hand configuration.

The 21 landmarks are distributed across the hand as follows: landmark 0 is the wrist, landmarks 1–4 cover the thumb from base to tip, landmarks 5–8 cover the index finger, landmarks 9–12 cover the middle finger, landmarks 13–16 cover the ring finger, and landmarks 17–20 cover the little finger. Each landmark has x, y, and z coordinates normalized relative to the image dimensions, giving a 63- dimensional feature vector per hand.

NumPy (Numerical Python) is a fundamental library for scientific computing in Python. It provides support for large, multi-dimensional arrays and matrices, along with a vast collection of mathematical functions to operate on these arrays. NumPy is the foundation upon which many other scientific
libraries, including TensorFlow and OpenCV, are built.
data processing, including stacking, reshaping, and slicing.
dramatically faster execution than pure Python, especially on large datasets.
NumPy is used to collect MediaPipe landmark coordinates into arrays, normalize the feature vectors,
and prepare input tensors for model inference. During
arrays store the complete feature matrix (samples × 63 features) and the corresponding label array, which are then passed to TensorFlow for training.

Pandas is a powerful, open-source data analysis and manipulation library for Python. It provides data structures like DataFrames and Series that make it easy to work with structured data from various sources, including CSV files, Excel spreadsheets, and SQL databases.
feature vectors and labels for the gesture dataset.
to save and load processed data.
understanding dataset distribution and class balance.

Pandas DataFrames are used to organize the extracted feature vectors and their associated labels into a tabular format. The dataset can be exported to CSV files for inspection and imported for subsequent training runs without re-extracting landmarks. This also facilitates dataset versioning and

Pickle is a built-in Python module that implements binary protocols for serializing and deserializing Python object structures. It enables saving complex Python objects, including trained machine learning models, to disk and loading them back without retraining.

After training, the Keras model is saved using Pickle as a .pkl file. During real-time detection, the saved model is loaded from disk using Pickle, enabling immediate inference without retraining. The label encoder (mapping integer indices to gesture names) is also serialized using Pickle for consistent label
The OS module provides a portable interface to operating system functionalities, while the Shutil
module offers high-level file operations. Together, they manage the file system operations required throughout the project lifecycle.
each gesture class (A–Z, 0–9) if it doesn't already exist, and to organize captured images into the appropriate folders. During training, OS functions are
images in batch. Shutil is used to clean or reorganize the dataset when needed.

The "Sign Language Detection System" is a project designed to bridge communication gaps by recognizing hand gestures and mapping them to corresponding alphabets in real-time. The system employs a multi-step process involving dataset creation, model training, and gesture recognition. Users first create a dataset by capturing hand images using a camera and labeling them with corresponding alphabets. These labeled images are stored systematically for training a machine learning model. Once trained, the classifier detects hand gestures and identifies them with accurate labels, showcasing the potential for real-world applications such as aiding speech-impaired individuals.

The dataset creation phase is critical as it forms the foundation of the entire system. A high-quality, diverse, and well-labeled dataset directly determines the accuracy and robustness of the trained model. This phase involves image capture, manual labeling, and organized storage.
Using a camera integrated into the system, users capture a predetermined number of hand gesture

images (typically 100–300 images per class). These images represent various alphabets of the ASL and digits 0–9 and serve as the input for the system. The images are captured in consistent lighting and positioning to ensure uniformity across the dataset, which improves training accuracy.

After capturing the images, users manually label each image with the corresponding alphabet or digit. For example, if a user captures an image of the gesture for the letter 'A', they label it accordingly by storing it in the designated folder 'A'. This labeling step ensures that the dataset contains meaningful associations between gestures and class labels.
or digit) has its own subfolder where all images corresponding to that gesture are saved. For example:

This organization simplifies the process of loading the dataset during the training phase and ensures efficient access. A total of 36 gesture classes (26 letters + 10 digits) are supported, with each class containing at least 200 images for adequate model generalization.

Once the dataset is prepared, the system moves to the model training stage. This involves loading the dataset, extracting hand landmarks using MediaPipe, building a feature matrix, training the neural network, and evaluating its performance.

The system reads the labeled images from the storage folders using OpenCV and NumPy. It preprocesses these images by converting them from BGR to RGB (as required by MediaPipe), and then passes each image through the MediaPipe Hands solution to extract 21 hand landmarks.
For each image, the 21 landmarks provide 3D coordinates

in a 63-dimensional feature vector per image. These feature vectors, along with their class labels, are stored in a Pandas DataFrame and exported to a CSV file for reproducibility. The feature matrix has dimensions: (total_samples × 63).
The model is trained with the Adam optimizer (learning rate = 0.001), categorical cross-entropy loss,
and an early stopping callback (patience = 10) to prevent overfitting. Training runs for up to 50 epochs with a batch size of 32 and a 20% validation split.
The following metrics are calculated to measure the model's performance:
After training, the model is saved to disk using Pickle (.pkl format) and optionally
as a Keras HDF5 file (.h5) for cross-platform compatibility. The label encoder (mapping integer class indices to gesture names) is also saved alongside the model.
labeling. This phase brings the entire system together, demonstrating its practical value.

The system initializes a live camera feed using OpenCV's VideoCapture. A real-time video window is displayed where users can perform hand gestures in front of the camera. The system processes each frame independently and produces a prediction within milliseconds.
uncertain predictions and reduce false positives.
up to two hands simultaneously for future extensions.

The data collection script uses OpenCV to open the webcam, display a live feed, and capture hand gesture images on user command. The user selects the gesture class (e.g., 'A') and the script saves the specified number of images to the corresponding folder.

The training script iterates over all gesture class folders, reads each image, extracts MediaPipe landmarks, builds a feature matrix, and trains the neural network. The trained model is saved for later
The real-time detection script loads the saved model, opens the webcam, and runs continuous inference on each captured frame. The predicted gesture label is displayed on screen in real time.

A structured testing methodology was applied to evaluate the Sign Language Detection System across multiple dimensions: unit testing of individual modules, integration testing of the full pipeline, and user acceptance testing (UAT) with actual hand gesture inputs.
extracted with x, y, z values

After verifying individual modules, integration testing confirmed that the complete pipeline—from webcam input through MediaPipe landmark extraction, model inference, and OpenCV visualization— functions correctly end-to-end. Specific integration test scenarios included:
verifying improved detection for the new class.
collection, verifying generalization to unseen individuals.
A small group of five volunteers participated in UAT sessions. Participants performed each of the 36 gesture classes and rated the system on the following criteria:

Participants noted the system performed best for gestures with highly distinct hand configurations and occasionally struggled to distinguish visually similar gestures such as 'M'/'N' and 'U'/'V'. These findings are consistent with the per-class accuracy metrics obtained during formal evaluation.

A detailed analysis of the trained model reveals important insights about the strengths and limitations of the current approach. Per-class metrics highlight which gesture classes are reliably recognized and

The MediaPipe + MLP approach achieves a favorable balance between accuracy, speed, and hardware accessibility. It outperforms raw CNN approaches on standard CPU hardware while avoiding the cost and inconvenience of data glove solutions.

and user interface through screenshots. The included images highlight various stages of the project, such as dataset creation, model training, and real-time hand gesture detection. These screenshots provide a detailed look into the system's workflow and demonstrate its practical implementation, offering insight into the project's design, features, and performance.

The screenshot above illustrates the system's ability to detect and classify the ASL gesture for the digit '0'. The hand is shown making a circular shape with all fingers touching the thumb tip. MediaPipe correctly identifies all 21 landmarks, and the trained model outputs the correct class with high confidence (> 95%). The green bounding box and label are overlaid on the live frame using OpenCV drawing functions.
The letter 'K' in ASL is formed by
extending the index and middle fingers upward while the thumb rests between them. This gesture is among the more complex ones in the dataset due to the subtle differentiation
in real time, demonstrating the model's ability to distinguish fine-grained differences in hand landmark
The letter 'J' involves a dynamic motion in actual ASL (a J-shaped path traced by the little finger). For this static detection system, the starting

handshape) is used as the representative gesture. This is an acknowledged limitation of the current system, which will be addressed in future work by incorporating temporal modeling for dynamic gesture recognition.

The screenshots above demonstrate the system's real-time detection capability. The left image shows a hand performing an ASL gesture, with the system correctly identifying and labeling it on the live video feed. The right image shows another gesture being detected from a slightly different angle and hand orientation, highlighting the system's robustness to minor variations in gesture execution.
the hand, even at varying distances from the camera.

The neural network model was trained over 50 epochs with early stopping (patience = 10). The training process was monitored through loss and accuracy curves to ensure healthy convergence and detect any signs of overfitting.

The training curves show a healthy convergence pattern: both training and validation accuracy improve steadily across epochs, and the gap between them remains small, indicating good generalization with minimal overfitting. The early stopping callback triggered at epoch 48 (after 10 consecutive epochs without validation loss improvement), preventing unnecessary computation and overfitting.

The model's performance varied across gesture classes, as expected. Static, highly distinct gestures achieved near-perfect accuracy, while gestures that are visually similar to others presented greater challenges. The following table presents the per-class F1-scores for a representative subset of classes:

T (which have visually similar handshapes). Future improvements to the dataset (more diverse samples) and model architecture (temporal modeling for dynamic gestures) are expected to improve scores for these challenging classes.
the similar thumb-over-fingers configuration with only one finger difference.
involve two extended fingers, differing only in whether they are together or apart.
These confusion patterns are consistent with the inherent visual ambiguity between specific ASL letter pairs and align with similar
dynamic gesture modeling for letters like 'J' and 'Z', and the need for larger, more varied datasets for visually similar letter pairs.
To contextualize the system's performance, the MLP classifier was compared against several baseline
The landmark-based MLP approach outperforms all baseline methods in accuracy while maintaining competitive

(ResNet-18) achieves a respectable 89.3% accuracy but requires significantly more training time, data, and computational resources. The landmark-based approach is therefore the most efficient choice for this
The system's real-time performance was benchmarked on two different hardware configurations to
assess its suitability for deployment on low-end and standard computing devices:
The results confirm that the system is functional on low-end hardware, though with reduced frame rates. On mid-range hardware (which represents the typical student or professional laptop), the system

operates comfortably above 24 FPS, which is generally considered the threshold for smooth video playback and real-time interaction. GPU acceleration provides a further significant improvement, making the system well-suited for demanding deployment environments.

The Sign Language Detection System developed in this project represents a significant step toward bridging the communication gap between individuals with hearing or speech impairments and the general population. Through the integration of computer vision, machine learning, and real-time video processing, the system successfully demonstrates the feasibility of automated, vision-based sign language recognition using widely available hardware and open-source software.
The project followed a systematic development approach comprising

phases: dataset creation, model training, and real-time gesture detection. Each phase was implemented as a modular, independent Python script, promoting maintainability and extensibility. The dataset of over 7,200 labeled hand gesture
36 gesture classes covering the ASL alphabet and digits.
MediaPipe's hand landmark extraction proved to be a highly effective feature engineering approach, transforming

effectively represent hand configuration. This landmark-based approach significantly reduces model complexity compared to raw pixel-based methods while maintaining high accuracy. The decision to use hand landmarks as features rather than raw pixel values was validated by the strong performance of the resulting model, as landmark-based representations are inherently invariant to background changes and largely robust to variations in image resolution.

The TensorFlow/Keras multi-layer perceptron classifier achieved an overall accuracy of 93.7% on the held-out test set, with strong precision and recall across most gesture classes. The system operates at approximately 28 FPS in real-time mode, providing smooth and responsive detection. Dropout regularization and early
stopping were effective in preventing overfitting, resulting in a well-generalized model that performs consistently on unseen gesture samples.
pipeline, from data collection to real-time inference.

Working on this project provided invaluable hands-on experience with the end-to-end machine learning pipeline, from data collection and preprocessing through model training, evaluation, and deployment. Key lessons learned include:
The quality and diversity of the training dataset had the single largest impact on model accuracy. Inconsistent image quality, poor lighting, or insufficient samples per class

Using MediaPipe's hand landmarks as features rather than raw pixel values dramatically reduced model training time, data requirements, and inference complexity. Good feature engineering can often outperform brute-force deep learning on limited datasets.
Without dropout layers and early stopping, the initial model significantly overfit the training data. Adding regularization techniques brought the training and validation loss
curves into alignment, indicating better generalization.
Separating the data collection, training, and inference components into independent scripts made it much easier to identify and fix issues at each stage without affecting other parts of the system.
The project also highlighted the broader social impact of AI-driven accessibility
the potential to improve daily communication for millions of hearing-impaired individuals worldwide, supporting greater inclusion in education, public services, and workplace environments.

In conclusion, the Sign Language Detection System achieves its stated objectives and lays a strong foundation for more advanced future developments. The technical skills, problem-solving experience, and domain knowledge gained through this project will serve as a valuable asset in the pursuit of further innovation in AI and assistive technology. The intersection of machine learning and accessibility represents one of the most impactful application domains for AI, and this project is a small but meaningful contribution to that space.

The Sign Language Detection System, as implemented in this project, demonstrates strong potential for future expansion. Several avenues exist for enhancing the system's capabilities, broadening its applicability, and integrating it into practical assistive technology
solutions. The future scope is outlined across multiple dimensions:

The current system is limited to static hand gestures, which restricts recognition to letters and digits that can be represented as fixed hand configurations. Many ASL letters (notably 'J' and 'Z') and most sign language words involve dynamic movements. Extending the system to recognize dynamic gestures would require temporal modeling using:
that process sequences of landmark frames over time.

Implementing dynamic gesture recognition would require collecting new datasets that capture gesture sequences as video clips rather than single images. Each clip would need to be labeled with the corresponding gesture and time-aligned to the key positions of the sign. A dataset of at least 500 video clips per dynamic gesture class would be recommended for robust training.
Moving beyond alphabet and digit recognition to full word and sentence-level sign language interpretation would significantly increase the system's practical utility. This would involve:
Integrating language models (such as GPT-based transformers) to improve contextual interpretation

The transition from fingerspelling recognition to full sign language interpretation represents the most impactful next step in this project's evolution. It would transform the system from a research prototype into a genuinely practical communication tool.

enabling one-way communication from a sign language user to a hearing individual. Python libraries such as pyttsx3 or Google Text-to-Speech (gTTS) can be integrated to convert recognized text into natural- sounding speech output in real time. Key considerations include:
The current system supports ASL only. Extending support to other sign languages would significantly
Indian Sign Language (ISL): Widely used by the deaf community across India, ISL has its own distinct
from ASL in both vocabulary and grammar.

Transfer learning from the existing ASL model could reduce the data and training requirements for new sign languages, particularly for handshapes that are similar across languages. Domain adaptation techniques could further accelerate the development of new language models.
more accessible and portable. This can be achieved through:
efficient inference on mobile and embedded devices with limited computational resources. TFLite supports post-training quantization, which further reduces model size and inference time.
cross- framework compatibility and deployment on non-Python runtimes.
model converted to Core ML format using Apple's coremltools library.
standalone kiosk deployment in public spaces.
require two-hand configurations. MediaPipe Hands already supports tracking up to two hands
(eyebrow raises, mouth shapes) that modify the meaning of signs in ASL and other sign languages.
positioning, enabling recognition of full-body signs that involve torso movement.
a unified multi-modal model for more accurate and complete sign language understanding.
As the system is applied in high-stakes accessibility contexts, understanding why the model makes a particular
modes. Integrating Explainable AI techniques would include:
SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations): Computing SHAP values to identify which hand landmarks
which parts of the input the model focuses on when making predictions.
a 90% confidence prediction is actually correct 90% of the time. Techniques like temperature scaling can be applied post-training.
explore misclassified samples, visualize confusion patterns, and identify data collection gaps.
A federated learning architecture could enable the system to improve continuously without centralizing private
server, which aggregates improvements and distributes enhanced model versions back to all instances. This
given the sensitive nature of assistive communication tools.
with different hand sizes, skin tones, and signing styles, improving generalization.
Video Conferencing: Integration as a browser extension or desktop plugin for platforms like Zoom,
language captions for uploaded videos, improving accessibility for deaf viewers.
Google Glass Enterprise Edition) to provide always-available, hands-free sign language detection for deaf users in public environments.
home automation devices (lights, thermostats, locks) through sign language gestures.

In summary, the Sign Language Detection System provides a solid, extensible foundation upon which a comprehensive, production-grade assistive communication platform can be built. The rapidly advancing capabilities of computer vision, deep learning, and mobile computing make the realization of these
future enhancements increasingly achievable within the coming years. Each enhancement brings the system
and deaf communities are eliminated through intelligent, accessible technology.
Molchanov, P., Yang, X., Gupta, S., Kim, K., Tyree, S., & Kautz, J. (2016). Online Detection and
Sequence Modelling with Deep Recurrent CNN-HMMs. Proceedings of CVPR 2017.
Beyond Temporal Pooling: Recurrence and Temporal Convolutions for Gesture Recognition in Video. International Journal of Computer Vision.
Transformers: Joint End-to-End Sign Language Recognition and Translation. Proceedings of CVPR 2020.
Sign Language Recognition Using MediaPipe Hand Tracking. Applied Sciences.
